sentences.text: Being that we arrived from Majdanek in very poor-- in a very poor situation, without having any assets to our own, just the clothes on our back, what was given to us, hungry.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It happened one day that I met at work a boy that approached me and asked me from where I am and where I was.
And when I told him from where I am, he somehow became a very good friend of mine.
He shared bread with me whenever I didn't have, because he had more than I had.
Because as I said, he came from home, and he had a lot of things, like bedding and clothes, because he came straight from home to this camp.
And he had his two brothers and his father and a sister were hidden by the Poles, not in camp.
And after a while, when we were in the camp, the Poles gave away to the German-- gave away his sister and his father, and they were brought into camp.
The father was a sick man, and he died into my-- my arms.
The sister was a beautiful young woman, and she had a child living with Poles, hidden by Poles.
But she was brought in, into camp.
And poor thing, she was running around in camp, screaming, where is my child?
And where is my child?
And one day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as we were marching out from camp to work, she was shot by the gate.
David and I, we became very good friends.
And as I said, he shared a lot of clothes and food with me, which made me easier survive the situation that I was in.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had no food, as I said.
At the end of the day, we were given the soup and the portion of bread.
But being that I had privilege, that David helped me with food, I was privileged to give my soup to some friends, that they need it.
I brought, every night after work, to a friend of mine that lives now in Boca, here.
That she was with me in Majdanek, and in Skarzysko, [INAUDIBLE], we were liberated together.
And she remembers because she had a very bad infection.
Was giving up, not to live.
And I brought her, every night, my portion of soup, into the back.
And David was my friend till they evacuated Skarzysko.
When they started evacuating Skarzysko, his brother that used to work with the doctors at that time for the camp, came to ask me that David shouldn't go into transport with me.
Because David insisted that he wants to go into transport with me.
We didn't know where we were really going again.
We didn't know that we are going to another [? hassock. ?] But he begged me that because he wants his brother to go with him.
Because the ones that they worked with the doctors were going with the next transport.
They didn't know where they were going either, but he wanted his brother to be with him.
But David insisted that he wanted to go with me in transport.
So he did.
He went with me to Tschenstochau, which was a very fatal move, because his brother went to Buchenwald, but he remained alive.
He lives now in Haifa, and I visited him in Israel.
But David, unfortunately, on the day that I was liberated, they evacuated, again, the camp.
And I was liberated that night, and David was-- they've taken into transport in the morning, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I never saw him again.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A friend of mine that was liberated that also worked with me and went in the same transport with David, came back-- he was liberated-- and told me that he witnessed David being shot.
They were-- they were being taken on a death march from one camp to another.
From Mauthausen, they were taken-- as the Russians were nearing, they were taking on a death march.
And it was winter.
And David sat down, and he said, if Miriam isn't-- I am sure that Miriam is not alive.
And if Miriam is not alive, I don't want to live either.
And he sat down, and he told Srulek-- that's the boy that told me-- that he doesn't want to live either.
And the SS that was leading the death march came up and shot him.
And after the war, when I was liberated, that man, Srulek, came and told me the news about David.
In fact, I had a lot of pictures of David that I brought with me to Haifa, to his brother, that his brother never had.
And I brought pictures.
Because David gave me pictures when we arrived to Tschenstochau, David gave me certain things that he took with him.
So he gave me for safekeeping.
Because he felt that I could keep them more discreet under my bunk.
So I kept them for him.
And when they took him away, I had all these things, and I was liberated with them.
And in Tschenstochau, we worked again in [? hassock. ?] But you see, in Tschenstochau, we had a different German woman over us, and she was very, very cruel.
I really don't know.
The other one was [NON-ENGLISH].
But here, sometimes we had a man.
One-- I beg your pardon?
The name of the camp commander, [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH]?
Hassock? ?]
No.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we worked there.
And you see, the German woman that was over us, she always used to say, [GERMAN].
It means, if you commit something, everybody has to pay for your sin, whatever you did.
Whether-- if we didn't make the norm of bullets, whatever we make, we were taken on the station and beaten.
The first time I had 25 lashes.
Not that I didn't do the norm, but every morning after the night shift, the bullets were counted.
You had to make a certain norm of bullets.
If one bullet was missing, the whole shift was taken for beating.
So we were taken for beating, 25 lashes.
We were taken in a barrack that was a special seat, that your head was bent between your legs.
And excuse me, I was all bare.
My bottom was all bare.
And we were beaten by a whip.
After 25 lashes, we were kicked out from the barrack.
One person.
One SS.
So when I had given the 25 lashes-- the first lashes, it was excruciating.
Then your skin becomes like leather, and you don't feel anything.
You could be beaten to death, because you don't feel anything.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And after the 25 lashes, we were marched, the best we could march, because we were all covered with welts.
Blood welts.
We were marched into the factory and lined up, and the German woman asked us, now you know why you were beaten.
Because next time, you will know to make the norm.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We worked in this factory.
Just before we were liberated, I was again taken for lashes.
But this time, I had 75 lashes.
Because again, somebody didn't make the norm.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And each one of us had 75 lashes.
When I was liberated, I couldn't walk.
I couldn't lay down.
I couldn't sit.
Because my whole body was black welts of blood.
It took months for me to heal after I was liberated.
But in Tschenstochau, we didn't know if we were going to come out alive.
But we heard that the Russian are getting near, because they were evacuating camps.
And one night, we heard bullets and ammunition flying over our heads.
We didn't know what goes on.
But the next morning, as I said, 6 o'clock in the morning, we heard that there were two transports taken out from camp.
We didn't know their destination.
And that was one transport that David went in.
And we were taken in special bunks for the next transport, to go into transport.
But they didn't have time to evacuate us, because the Russians took over our camp, and we were liberated; the 16th of January, 1945.
Yes.
Not-- not exactly.
Unless you know a certain person that could give you a certain vitamins or whatever.
For instance, in Skarzysko, I had pyorrhea in my teeth.
And my whole mouth was full of pus.
Whatever food I had, I couldn't eat, because I had a temperature, and my mouth was so sore and swollen and full of pus.
And they-- it was David's brother that gave me certain vitamins, or it was medication.
I don't know.
That healed me to a certain point that I was able to survive.
That I still had left my teeth in my mouth.
Like a first aid.
I really didn't know if they were really doctors, but it was like a barracks set up, like, for the first aid for certain moments.
I think the most difficult was the Konzentrationslager in Majdanek.
To witness throngs, and lines, and lines of people going to their death.
And the smell, constantly, from the crematoria.
And to witness this daily and knowing that so many thousands of people are going to their deaths.
And knowing that we will never come out, because we never had hopes that we would ever come out alive from this camp.
In Skarzysko.
I had a Pole.
His name was Stempinsky.
He used to come every morning to work in camp.
He lived on the Polish side, but he worked in camp for the Nazis.
And he was a Pole, and he always used-- and I was working under him.
He was like a-- he was [NON-ENGLISH].
That means he was the-- like the manager from the [NON-ENGLISH].
I used to work by the [NON-ENGLISH], in [NON-ENGLISH].
And he was like the manager in that [NON-ENGLISH].
And he always was very helpful by bringing me an extra piece of bread.
Or when he was in charge to give out the soup in the evening, he always gave me, from the bottom, a little bit thicker, with that extra piece of potato in it.
And that meant survival for us; that extra piece of bread or that extra piece of potato meant surviving at that time.
So the only Pole that I witnessed, that he was the nicest that I came across too.
That he was helpful.
Not really.
We didn't think that it's going to be-- that we're going to be liberated.

In fact, two days before we were liberated, there was going around a rumor in camp that they're going to dynamite the whole factory, and the Germans will dynamite the whole camp, that nobody should be a witness.
And we were very afraid.
And when they started to evacuate in the morning, the people with the first two transports, we knew that something goes on that we will not come out alive.
Either we're going to go into transport, or we'll be finished off right here.
And when we were taken to the bunks, they told us that we were waiting for the trains, to go on the next transport.
We never knew if we're going to be liberated, or we're going to be alive, or we-- we're going to be shot in there.
Or dynamite.
But the Russians came there, and we were liberated.
They didn't have time to-- do something with us.
In fact, when the Russians came into the city, they took so much revenge on the Germans and the Poles, that the things that we witnessed, it was unbelievable.
They really took revenge for us.
For all of us.
They used to shoot them in the middle of the street.
They used to cut off their fingers, to take off their rings.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I, myself, with my girlfriend, the first two weeks, slept on the-- in the attic-- in the attic, because the Russians, when they walk-- walked in, into our camp, they kind of were very wild.
Because they always used to say in Russian that [RUSSIAN].
That meant that you were collaborating for the Germans.
You made their ammunition, and you live with them, and you don't want to collaborate with us.
So we were afraid, because they did a lot of sexual harassment for the girls.
So my girlfriend and I, we hid into-- in the attic for the first two weeks, because the city-- my city was not liberated yet.
So we were waiting till more cities were liberated.
Because don't forget, we were liberated on January 16.
That it was still early.
A lot of camps were still-- so many thousands and thousands of Jews died until May 6 in other camps until they were liberated.
And I was liberated January 16.
So then I heard that Lodz was liberated.
And at night, we came out from-- from the attic-- Who did you hear from?
I beg your pardon?
At night, we used to go down for food.
To different homes, to empty homes.
For water.
For food.
So we heard that Lodz was liberated.
And at night, my girlfriend and I, we went to the train, and we went to Lodz.
At that time, we weren't scared at all.
From nothing.
We had no money.
We had no nothing with us.
The things that we were liberated.
We had nothing.
Camp clothes.
Clothes, like a dress with a rope around me, because it was too long.
In fact, they were giving me that dress.
It was so long that my girlfriend-- she was very good in sewing at that time-- she made me the first bra from that piece of material, because I started to become a woman in camp.
And she made me from this material a bra.
My first bra.
No.
We didn't.
She cut off a piece from my dress.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In fact, just before we were liberated, both of us got our period back.
And from a piece from her shirt, she got a long shirt, she made like a napkin holder.
Like a belt.
To hold pieces for us.
When I was finished, she started.
So we both alternated.
As I said, I went towards a train to Lodz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I still lived with a dream that when I come back to my hometown, that I would find somebody from my family.
So I went to my hometown, and I went back to Lodz.
When I came off the train, I met a friend of my mother's that arrived there before me.
And he recognized me, and he already had an apartment with other few boys.
And he took me and my girlfriend to his-- to their apartment.
When I came there, and that man was from [NON-ENGLISH].
They had formed a [NON-ENGLISH] for the Jews, that every morning, by [NON-ENGLISH], they posted a big list.
Whoever was liberated posted their name, that we should be able to see if we find somebody from the families.
And every morning, my girlfriend and I, we went to the [NON-ENGLISH] to see if anybody became liberated.
At one point, I received a letter in Lodz.
I received a letter from Skarzysko, from David's brother, Moshe, who lives now in Haifa, telling me that I should please come to Skarzysko by train, because he heard news that David is alive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I left my girlfriend [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Lodz, and I told her, you stay put.
When I find David, I'll come back, and we'll stay in Lodz, all of us, together.
I boarded a train to Skarzysko, and I went back to Skarzysko.
And David's brother and an aunt who was liberated by Poles, an elderly woman, was liberated.
Met me by the train.
And I stayed by them a few days, by her aunt.
By the aunt.
I stayed there a few days, till that fellow Srulek came back, was liberated, and told me the bad news, that David is no more alive.
And that day, I boarded a train back to Lodz.
And I arrived to Lodz, and I couldn't find my girlfriend anymore, because she went with another group out of Poland.
And I boarded a train to [NON-ENGLISH] the same night, all by myself.
I went to [NON-ENGLISH].

From [NON-ENGLISH], I went to [NON-ENGLISH], [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and to the border that was the Polish-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] between Czechoslovakia-- the border between Poland and Czechoslovakia.
I came to [NON-ENGLISH], and they arrested me.
The Polish Army arrested me and put me back on a train back to Poland.
I started to cry.
And at that time, I had an [NON-ENGLISH], a document.
My name was-- from the concentration camp, my name was [NON-ENGLISH].
And having that document, I started to cry, and they allowed me to go into the office.
Because with my Polish language, I came to the office, and I pretended that I am a Pole, not Jewish.
And I said to them, what do you think, I'm a Polish citizen?
And do you think that I tried to run away from Poland?
This is my country.
I am only going because I found out that my father is in Prague.
I am coming back, because I'm a Polish citizen.
And they let me go back to the train to go to Czechoslovakia.
And I arrived this way to Prague.

When I arrived to Prague, it was the IRO, the international Refugee Organization, the IRO, and the Jewish Federation, and the UNRWA took care-- there was also the [? gemeente, ?] and there was all the officers.
And I went there, and we were fed.
And I met a certain someone in-- in the Jewish Federation that remembered me from Lodz.
My family.
And they said that they were liberated to [NON-ENGLISH] a few weeks ago, and they thought that they saw my father there.
And I boarded a train that same night to [NON-ENGLISH], from Prague.
And when I arrived to [NON-ENGLISH], I uncovered the thousands of bodies, looking for my father.
But unfortunately, I couldn't find anybody.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was there two days.
And after two days, I went back to Prague.
From Prague, we formed a whole group of young girls and boys.
And from the Jewish Federation, from the UNRWA, they transported us to Salzburg, Austria.
And that was a displaced persons camp in Austria.
When we arrived into camp in Austria, we had food.
It was a novelty to us, because after the liberation, whatever came under my hand, I ate.
And we came into camp in Salzburg, and we were all young boys and girls my age.
And we were there a few weeks, that came a group of [HEBREW] from Israel.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they all introduced ourselves to us.
And they gave us lectures, and they said to us, you are not [NON-ENGLISH], my kids.
You have a land of Palestine.
At that time, Israel was not a state yet.
But they gave us hope to go to Israel.
